title: Was the Saber-Toothed Cat Doomed by Its Signature Fangs?
gist: Despite the headline's framing about Smilodon fatalis, the article's actual reported finding is that UC Berkeley postdoc Narimane Chatar re-identified a long-uncatalogued Arizona museum skull as Adelphailurus kansensis — a smaller-fanged relative — by matching it to a 1924 Kansas specimen; the text does not explain why Smilodon's own giant canines may have contributed to its extinction.

themes.theme: A misidentified museum skull rewrites the record

insights.claim: UC Berkeley postdoc Narimane Chatar re-examined an Arizona feline skull in the American Museum of Natural History's collections — previously tagged only as a generic "feline" — and noticed its canines were laterally compressed, indicating it was not a true cat or tiger.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: A reminder that museum backlogs of uncatalogued or misidentified specimens remain a source of new paleontological findings without new fieldwork.
insights.quote: In the drawer I also found upper canines… When I saw that they were laterally compressed, I knew it was not a cat or a tiger.
insights.locator: University of California, Berkeley postdoc Narimane Chatar

insights.claim: A 3-D reconstruction of the Arizona skull showed it closely resembles a 1924 Kansas specimen, uniting both as Adelphailurus kansensis, a puma-sized cat that inhabited western North America more than 5 million years ago.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Extends the known geographic range and timeline of this saber-toothed lineage and links two previously separate museum specimens into a single species record, published in the Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology.
insights.locator: After creating a 3-D reconstruction of the entire skull

insights.claim: Adelphailurus kansensis had fangs about an inch long — far shorter than the famous Smilodon fatalis' 7-inch canines — yet its serrated-edge teeth likely still let it cut through flesh like a blade.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Suggests extreme canine length was not a prerequisite for an effective saber-tooth predatory strategy, a data point relevant to evolutionary debates about the trade-offs behind Smilodon's giant fangs.
insights.quote: its fangs were much shorter than those of the iconic S. fatalis but could still likely cut through flesh like a blade
insights.locator: The long, narrow snout and serrated-edge teeth
